text: I'm going to speak to you over the next short while about laparoscopic total proctocolectomy and brook ileostomy in the treatment of inflammatory bowel disease. The indications for this operation are several, in general being far more common in patients with Crohn's disease than in patients with mucosal cervical colitis or familial adenomatous polyposis.

But certainly the operation can be used in both groups of patients. And the patients with Crohn's disease, in general, they're patients whose rectum is pretty much unusable, poor capacity, poor compliance from chronic inflammation, and or severe perianal disease.

Once in a while, someone with bad proctitis with no perianal disease may prefer a subtotal colectomy, ileostomy, see if we can aggressively treat the rectum, with some topical compounds and then do an ilioproctostomy at a subsequent date.

But if the patient also has severe perianal disease, unfortunately that ends up being an interesting teric dissection. There is also the indication in patients who have a pretty reasonable rectum, but a typical watering pot perineum with severe fistulizing disease where total proctocolectomy ends up being the operation of choice.

In patients with ulcerative colitis, it is seldom that they would select a total proctocolectomy if truly well-informed about all the options, but there are some instances. Patients with very low resting pressures who may be incontinent may prefer that option.

Patients who are very elderly may not have the sort of stamina, if you will, to withstand the operation of a J-pouch if that operation were accompanied by any postoperative adverse sequelae. And then there's the group in whom you try a pouch and you just simply can't make one, but the patient didn't want a continental ileostomy.

Then there's this odd group of indeterminate colitis, although that's far less seldom. There are a couple of studies looking at total abdominal colectomies performed laparoscopically, as well as pouches performed laparoscopically. There's not a huge amount in the literature, nothing like the volume we see for sigmoid colitis, ileocolic resection for Crohn's, cancer operations.

fairly less common in large measure due to the magnitude of the operation and the technical difficulties attended with it. We had published in nineteen ninety-two on laparoscopic total abdominal colectomies that we'd started performing sixteen years ago in nineteen ninety-one and in this initial study we discussed a group of patients who'd undergone either standard laparotomy with total abdominal colectomy and ileoprotostomy

or ilioanal reservoir and we looked at a similar albeit quite small group at the time in nineteen ninety two publications a group who underwent the same operations but performed laparoscopically in that very early study from nineteen ninety one operations performed laparoscopically we looked at the typical parameters for length of surgery length of ileus length of hospitalization morbidity and mortality

And again, they were small groups, five patients in each group, pretty well matched by age, by gender, as well as by indication for surgery. The two patients with colonic inertia underwent abdominal colectomelial proctostomy and not pouched. And in that early group, you can see that the length of surgery was longer laparoscopically. The length of ileus was not any better.

The length of hospital stay was not really dramatically better, and there was really no significant morbidity or mortality. So at that point, we were able to say it seems feasible. We don't seem to be doing the patients any significant harm, no recognizable benefits, but at least it was kind of a green light to continue on.

A decade later, there's obviously a larger series in the literature, and this one from the Journal of Sages, Surgical Endoscopy, included seventy-three patients who underwent total abdominal colectomy with iliorectal, performed laparoscopic or open, as well as twenty-eight, again a much smaller group, undergoing the operation we're primarily discussing this morning, total proctoclectomy and brook ileostomy, fifteen laparoscopic, thirteen open, with no differences in gender, age, or weight in either of the two groups.

If we look at the laparoscopic versus open, specifically in the total proctocolectomy series, we have twenty-eight patients in total, no differences, again, gender, no differences in age, median weight, or indication for surgery being almost evenly distributed between Crohn's disease and mucosal sort of colitis.

The operative time was still much longer, as we'd found it a decade earlier, a mean of four hundred minutes and up to ten hours. Now again, this is one of the reasons that we don't see, or one of several reasons, we don't see as many series in the literature on laparoscopic total proctocolectomy as we see on laparoscopic sigmoid or laparoscopic ileocolic.

Most people can't afford to spend eight to ten hours in the OR and shouldn't. And most operating rooms wouldn't generally allow it from a cost point of view, if nothing else. And what's more, at the end of it, if we look at the length of stay, it's eight days instead of nine days. It's not a dramatic gain like we see in other areas.
Granted, we're not seemingly doing any harm to the patients in terms of an increased incidence of perioperative complications or mortality. but the length of stay is just not dramatically decreased.

If we look at the patients available for follow-up and we look at the median follow-up, the satisfaction with the operation, short-term and long-term complications, again, they're not dramatically different between the two groups. So it becomes somewhat hard to justify doing the operation other than to say we can't.

Now, of course, the caveat is if you do a lot of these, if you happen to have a practice that is a tertiary or floral inflammatory bowel disease practice, and you're doing a lot of these, you may be able to get a different result than if you're doing the occasional total proctocolectomy, prokiliostomy. I'll show you some technical facets of the operation momentarily that do make it nicely suited for laparoscopy if you're in the vicinity of doing

fifty to a hundred or more ulcerative colitis and Crohn's patients per year with either J-pouch or total proctocolectomy. This is Peter Marcello's paper from Disease of the Colon and Rectum, published the same year as the surgical endoscopy study, and these patients underwent total colectomy.

for colitis in the acute setting. Slightly different group of patients than in the chronic setting. The chronic patients tending to be more refractory to medical management, the acute obviously being by definition acutely ill. But you can see the methodology in the manuscript. No differences, again, in age, in the gender distribution, in the body mass index, or in the diagnosis.

And one thing I will draw your attention to, that in many of the laparoscopic series, the patients tend to be on the thin side. Mean BMI of twenty-four, they're reasonably well selected cases. It's not a mean of thirty, as we might see if we just randomly selected patients from any of our practices in North America or in Western Europe.

The operative time, and this is in the hands of a master laparoscopic surgeon, somebody very much respected by this society and others, but still the operative time is virtually doubled, even in those hands. The return of bowel function, a little bit quicker, and length of stay is two days less. So there's some gain here, but at what price, doubling the length of OR, I'm not sure.

In terms of morbidity, there's no significant gain there. So, again, it just keeps being the same conclusion, whether it's in the year, it's feasible, it's safe, it may lead to a faster recovery. And more recently, as we've described at this meeting last year, an incisionless proctectomy with transperineal colonic J pouch.

This is an incisionless, totally laparoscopic proctoclectomy with brocoliostomy. which in fact is the way it's been performed since nineteen ninety-one. So again, we see the same words over and over and over again in every series, feasible, safe, feasible, safe, you know, maybe some slight benefits. The corollary is the laparoscopic abdominal perineal resection, and there's certainly a lot more series in the literature describing it.

In this two thousand six review in the Annals of Surgical Oncology, you can see a variety of series in which laparoscopic APR was described, and you can kind of use that as a jumping off point for laparoscopic total proctocolectomy. But what I'd like to do is actually show you the technique, at least the one that we've been using for the last sixteen years.
The surgeon uses a peri-umbilical port for the laparoscopic camera, moving it frequently from left to right and right to left during the total proctocolectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses Babcock forceps to demonstrate the location of the ureter in the right lower quadrant. A 5mm harmonic scalpel is employed to perform the line of TOLT mobilization. The patient's head is oriented towards the right side of the screen, while the feet are towards the left, with the right ureter reflected down.

The surgeon uses two ports on the left side for right-hand mobilization, creating a mirror image operation with ports in the left upper and lower quadrants for right side dissection, and ports in the right upper and lower quadrants for left side dissection. The harmonic scalpel is used to dissect around the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon uses two contralateral ports to facilitate dissection, occasionally adding an extra instrument for traction. The right ureter is exposed and cleared of surrounding tissue. The dissection progresses towards the hepatic flexure, with vessel division for the total proctocolectomy occurring at varying times.

If the patient's very thin and the transverse mesocolon is fused with the gastrocolic momentum, I tend to do it at this stage in the operation. If they're a bit fatter, I'll tend to take down the gastrocolic momentum either through the vascular plane or dividing the momentum itself and then come back. So we want to see the duodenum as an endpoint, obviously.

The surgeon uses the ENSEAL instrument to dissect the gastrocolic omentum, applying energy to separate the tissue. Subsequently, the transverse mesocolon is taken with an endoscopic linear cutter equipped with a vascular load stapler.

The spleen is visible in the left upper quadrant of the screen as the surgeon approaches the splenic flexure. The right colon has been mobilized, and the surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel for precise dissection along the left side of the colon. The left ureter is identified as the dissection progresses from the rectosigmoid junction towards the splenic flexure, with the line of Toldt being mobilized intracorporeally using laparoscopic instruments.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic dissection along the left side of the colon, with the small bowel visible under the mesentery as vessels are divided using energy sources. The procedure avoids incisions other than at the perineum and stoma site, focusing on staying close to the colon wall rather than approaching more proximal vessels.

The surgeon uses a vascular load and the ENSEAL instrument to connect the left and right colon up towards the splenic flexure. The left colon is visible under the left colonic mesentery and the marginal arcade. The middle colic artery is identified as the last structure to be taken, following the use of three vascular loads for the inferior mesenteric, iliocolic, and middle colic arteries. The entire abdominal colon is now mobilized.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter and prepares to take the vessels along the terminal ileum to fashion the ileostomy. The approach to vessel division depends on the extraction method, either through the stoma site or intracorporeally.

Once in a while the staplers don't work perfectly and we'll go ahead and put on a clip afterwards. Or once in a while the clips don't work and we put on an endo-loop. This is a fairly reasonable example of a variety of resourceful maneuvers to get some hemostasis without having to do anything else. You know, everything should always be there and in the room. And I must say, I never let my left hand holding that Babcock off until I'm happy with hemostasis on a vessel. And this, fortunately, was not a proximal ligation. This is benign disease. I'm not the least bit concerned with it.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis using an endo-loop after the stapler and clip failed. The dissection proceeds into the pelvis and pre-sacral space, entering the avascular plane between the sacrum and mesorectum. The areolar tissue is visible posteriorly, with the anus oriented towards the top midline of the screen and the patient's left side towards the left.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the left side, working in a circular motion around the presacral space while avoiding the mesorectum. Traction and counter-traction are applied to expose the areolar plane. A small nerve twig is visible on the left-hand side of the screen, which the surgeon reflects laterally.

The surgeon navigates around a large uterus, using a clamp to hold it up and reflect it out of the way, while working in the anterior plane. A nerve is visible on the left side, preserved despite the challenging anatomy.

The surgeon attempts to reflect the uterus towards the left-hand side to maintain clear visualization of the surgical field. The camera is repositioned through the lower quadrant port to assist in holding the uterus out of the way. The surgical team works anteriorly, carefully avoiding the uterus, and proceeds towards the right side, preparing to transition to the perineal phase of the procedure.

The surgeon transects the rectum and prepares to remove the specimen through the perineum. A blue load is used to create an ileostomy, ensuring the vascular pedicle on the iliocolic arcade is hemostatic. The ileum is positioned to reach the ileostomy site without tension. The procedure transitions to the perineal side for an intersphincteric proctectomy, with the specimen visibly extracted and the stoma pulled through the port site for maturation of the Brooke ileostomy.

The surgeon navigates the intersphincteric plane, with the uterus no longer obstructing the view. The entire colon, including the omentum and terminal ileum, is extracted through this plane. The right colon is visible as it emerges, and the remaining specimen is removed. The procedure resembles natural orifice surgery, with the final steps involving the extraction and maturation of the ileostomy.
